title: Path Planning Fundamentals
objective: Understand the major path planning algorithms used in robotics, from grid-based search to sampling-based planners, and how global and local planning work together for autonomous navigation.
beginnerExplanation:
Once a robot knows where it is (localisation) and has a map of its environment (mapping), it needs to figure out how to get from where it is to where it wants to go. This is path planning — finding a collision-free route through the environment.

The simplest approach is to overlay a grid on the map and use a graph search algorithm. A* (A-star) is the most famous and widely used. It searches outward from the start position, exploring cells that seem most promising — those that are close to the goal and have not accumulated too much cost to reach. A* is guaranteed to find the shortest path if one exists, and it does so efficiently by using a heuristic (an estimate of remaining distance to the goal) to guide the search toward the goal rather than searching in all directions equally.

But a grid search is just the first step. The path A* finds is typically a series of grid cell waypoints — jagged, with sharp corners that a real robot cannot follow. A smoothing step converts this into a curve that the robot's motion controller can actually execute. Additionally, the grid resolution creates a trade-off: fine grids give better paths but are computationally expensive; coarse grids are fast but may miss narrow passages.

Real robots use a two-level planning architecture. The global planner (like A*) finds a route through the entire map — the big picture. The local planner handles real-time obstacle avoidance over the next few metres, reacting to dynamic obstacles (people walking, other robots) that were not in the original map. The Dynamic Window Approach (DWA) is a popular local planner that considers the robot's velocity and acceleration limits to find commands that avoid immediate obstacles while making progress along the global path.
deeperExplanation:
Path planning in robotics extends well beyond 2D grid search. The general problem is finding a collision-free path through configuration space (C-space) — the space of all possible robot configurations. For a mobile robot on a floor, C-space is 2D or 3D (x, y, heading). For a 6-DOF robot arm, C-space is 6-dimensional. For a humanoid robot, it can be 30+ dimensions. Planning in high-dimensional C-space is fundamentally challenging because the computational cost of grid-based methods grows exponentially with dimension.

Sampling-based planners address this curse of dimensionality. Rapidly-exploring Random Trees (RRT) work by randomly sampling points in C-space and connecting them to the nearest existing tree node, growing a tree from the start toward the goal. RRT* is an optimised variant that rewires the tree to find shorter paths as more samples are drawn. Probabilistic Roadmaps (PRM) take a different approach: they pre-compute a network of collision-free paths by sampling random configurations and connecting nearby ones, then search this roadmap for paths between any start and goal. These algorithms are probabilistically complete — given enough time, they will find a path if one exists — and work efficiently in high-dimensional spaces.

Potential field methods offer an intuitive alternative. The goal generates an attractive field (pulling the robot toward it), and obstacles generate repulsive fields (pushing the robot away). The robot follows the gradient of the combined field. This is computationally cheap and produces smooth paths, but it can get stuck in local minima — saddle points where the attractive and repulsive forces balance, leaving the robot motionless. Various escape strategies (random perturbation, sub-goals) mitigate this limitation.

For autonomous vehicles and large mobile robots, lattice-based planners are common. They discretise the space of feasible motions (curves the vehicle can actually follow, given steering constraints) into a lattice of motion primitives. Planning then becomes a graph search over this lattice, guaranteeing that the resulting path is dynamically feasible — the vehicle can actually follow it given its turning radius and velocity constraints.

The Navigation2 (Nav2) stack in ROS 2 implements this global-local planning architecture as a configurable framework. It includes multiple global planners (NavFn for A*-based planning, Smac for lattice-based planning), multiple local planners (DWA, TEB, MPPI), a costmap system that inflates obstacles with safety margins, and a behaviour tree that orchestrates the interaction between planners, recovery behaviours, and goal management. Nav2 is the standard navigation stack used by most ROS 2-based mobile robots.

In Australian mining, path planning for autonomous haul trucks must handle additional complexity: one-way haul roads, priority intersections, grade changes that affect braking distance, and dynamic obstacles like light vehicles and personnel. The planning system must also optimise for fuel efficiency — the optimal path considers both distance and elevation change, preferring loaded downhill segments where possible.
keyTakeaways: A* is the foundational graph search algorithm for path planning — efficient and optimal for grid-based maps
Sampling-based planners (RRT, PRM) handle high-dimensional configuration spaces where grid-based methods fail
Global planners find the overall route; local planners handle real-time obstacle avoidance
The Dynamic Window Approach considers the robot's physical motion constraints for local planning
Nav2 in ROS 2 provides a complete, configurable navigation stack used by most mobile robots
Real-world planning must account for vehicle dynamics, terrain, and dynamic obstacles
example: An autonomous forklift in an Australian warehouse uses a two-level planning architecture. The global planner uses A* on a 2D occupancy grid (built by SLAM during a mapping run) to plan a route from the loading dock to storage aisle 14. The path is about 120 metres, weaving through aisles and around fixed shelving. As the forklift drives, the local planner (DWA) continuously adjusts the velocity commands, slowing down around corners, maintaining safe distance from shelves, and stopping when a human worker steps into the aisle ahead. The costmap inflates all obstacles by the forklift's width plus a safety margin, ensuring the planned paths maintain clearance. When the worker moves out of the way, the local planner smoothly resumes navigation. The entire trip takes about 3 minutes, compared to 2 minutes for a human driver — slightly slower, but the forklift operates 24 hours a day without breaks.
commonMistakes: Using A* in high-dimensional spaces — the computational cost is prohibitive above 3-4 dimensions; use sampling-based planners instead
Planning paths without considering robot dynamics — a path with sharp right-angle turns is useless for a car-like vehicle that cannot turn on the spot
Setting obstacle inflation too small — the robot clips corners and collides; too large and it cannot fit through doorways
Relying solely on a global plan without local replanning — the map is never perfectly accurate, and new obstacles appear
Not handling planning failure gracefully — when no path exists, the robot should report the failure rather than drive into walls
exercise: Implement A* pathfinding in Python on a simple 2D grid. Create a 20x20 grid with some cells marked as obstacles. Implement A* with the Euclidean distance heuristic to find the shortest path from the top-left corner to the bottom-right corner. Visualise the grid, obstacles, explored cells, and final path using matplotlib. Then experiment: what happens when you change the heuristic to Manhattan distance? What happens when you add more obstacles to create a maze? Can A* still find a path? This exercise builds intuition for how search-based planning works.

quiz.question: What is the key advantage of A* over Dijkstra's algorithm for path planning?
options: A* always finds a shorter path
A* uses a heuristic to guide search toward the goal, exploring fewer cells
A* works in 3D while Dijkstra's only works in 2D
A* can handle dynamic obstacles while Dijkstra's cannot
quiz.explanation: Both A* and Dijkstra's find the optimal shortest path, but A* uses a heuristic function (an estimate of the remaining distance to the goal) to prioritise exploring cells that are in the general direction of the goal. This means A* typically explores far fewer cells than Dijkstra's (which searches uniformly in all directions), making it significantly faster in practice.

quiz.question: Why do mobile robots typically use both a global planner and a local planner?
options: The global planner is for indoor use and the local planner is for outdoor use
The global planner finds the overall route on the map while the local planner handles real-time obstacle avoidance
The global planner is faster and the local planner is more accurate
The global planner works with LiDAR and the local planner works with cameras
quiz.explanation: The global planner computes a route through the entire map, considering the overall layout of the environment. The local planner operates in real time over a short horizon (a few metres ahead), adjusting the robot's velocity and steering to avoid dynamic obstacles (people, other robots) and handle map inaccuracies that the global planner could not anticipate. This two-level architecture provides both strategic routing and reactive safety.

perspectives.navigationEngineer: As a navigation engineer, you configure and tune the planning stack — selecting planners, setting costmap parameters, defining recovery behaviours, and testing in simulation and on hardware across diverse environments.
perspectives.algorithmResearcher: As an algorithm researcher, you develop new planning methods that are faster, more optimal, or handle new constraints — kinodynamic planning, multi-robot coordination, planning under uncertainty.
perspectives.fleetManager: As a fleet manager overseeing multiple autonomous vehicles, you care about system-level planning: traffic management, route optimisation across the fleet, throughput maximisation, and deadlock avoidance.
